paper_title: Test-Time Model Adaptation with Only Forward Passes
paper_abstract:
Test-time adaptation has proven effective in adapting a given trained model
to unseen test samples with potential distribution shifts. However, in
real-world scenarios, models are usually deployed on resource-limited devices,
e.g., FPGAs, and are often quantized and hard-coded with non-modifiable
parameters for acceleration. In light of this, existing methods are often
infeasible since they heavily depend on computation-intensive backpropagation
for model updating that may be not supported. To address this, we propose a
test-time Forward-Optimization Adaptation (FOA) method. In FOA, we seek to
solely learn a newly added prompt (as model's input) via a derivative-free
covariance matrix adaptation evolution strategy. To make this strategy work
stably under our online unsupervised setting, we devise a novel fitness
function by measuring test-training statistic discrepancy and model prediction
entropy. Moreover, we design an activation shifting scheme that directly tunes
the model activations for shifted test samples, making them align with the
source training domain, thereby further enhancing adaptation performance.
Without using any backpropagation and altering model weights, FOA runs on
quantized 8-bit ViT outperforms gradient-based TENT on full-precision 32-bit
ViT, while achieving an up to 24-fold memory reduction on ImageNet-C.

techniques.name: Forward-Optimization Adaptation
techniques.description: Forward-Optimization Adaptation is an end-to-end test-time adaptation paradigm that operates exclusively with forward passes and without any gradient backpropagation or model weight updates. For each incoming batch of out-of-distribution (OOD) test samples (e.g., with batch sizes of 2 or 4), it simultaneously learns a small set of prompt embeddings at the model’s input by employing a covariance matrix adaptation evolution strategy (CMA-ES) driven by a novel unsupervised fitness function and refines the final CLS token activations through a back-to-source activation shifting mechanism. The CMA-ES optimizer treats the prompt embeddings as a low-dimensional black-box parameter vector and evaluates candidate solutions using a fitness score that combines the model’s prediction entropy with the discrepancy between the OOD CLS activations and precomputed source CLS activation statistics, the latter estimated once from a small pool of in-distribution (ID) samples (32 samples suffice for ImageNet). Concurrently, the activation shifting module enacts a forward-only adjustment of the penultimate-layer features—prior to the final MLP head—by moving them toward the center of the source distribution along a dynamically updated direction. This unified strategy of input-level prompt adaptation and output-feature-level activation alignment delivers strong OOD generalization on both full-precision and quantized models with minimal memory and compute overhead.

components.name: CMA-Based Prompt Adaptation
components.description: In CMA-based prompt adaptation, a sequence of N_p learnable prompt embeddings p∈ℝ^{d×N_p} is prepended to each test batch X_t and only these embeddings are updated via a derivative-free covariance matrix adaptation evolution strategy (CMA-ES) while all model parameters remain frozen. The method maintains at iteration t a search distribution over prompts parameterized by a mean vector m^(t)∈ℝ^{dN_p}, a covariance matrix Σ^(t)∈ℝ^{dN_p×dN_p} and a global step size τ^(t)∈ℝ_+, which are initialized as m^(0)=0, Σ^(0)=I and τ^(0)=1. At each test iteration t, CMA-ES samples a population of K candidate prompts p_k^(t)∼m^(t)+τ^(t)𝒩(0,Σ^(t)), each of which is concatenated to X_t and forwarded through the frozen ViT model f_Θ. For each candidate, the model computes layer-wise CLS features and a final prediction ŷ_t^k, from which an unsupervised fitness v_k is computed according to a composite objective comprising the summed prediction entropy over the batch and a distribution alignment term weighted by λ that penalizes the ℓ₂ distance between the batch’s CLS activation statistics (means μ_i(X_t), standard deviations σ_i(X_t)) and the precomputed source-domain statistics (μ_i^S,σ_i^S). These fitness values are then used to rank the candidates and update m,Σ and τ via the standard CMA-ES likelihood maximization scheme. The prompt with the highest fitness in the resulting population is selected to generate the final prediction for batch X_t.

components.name: Unsupervised Fitness Function
components.description:
The unsupervised fitness function evaluates a candidate prompt p on an incoming test batch Xₜ by combining an entropy minimization term and an activation distribution discrepancy term. Specifically, letting ŷ = f_Θ(p; x) denote the class-probability vector produced by the transformer classifier head for each sample x in Xₜ, the function first computes the total prediction entropy ∑_{x∈Xₜ}∑_{c∈C} −ŷ_c log ŷ_c. To regularize against noisy out-of-distribution predictions, it enforces alignment between the CLS-token activation statistics of the test batch and pre-computed source in-distribution statistics. Before test-time adaptation, a small set of Q unlabeled ID samples (e.g. Q = 32 for ImageNet) is fed through the encoder to extract layer-wise CLS tokens e_i⁰ and compute their means μ_i^S and standard deviations σ_i^S for each transformer layer i ∈ {1…N}. During adaptation, the batch-wise CLS activations yield μ_i(Xₜ) and σ_i(Xₜ), and the L₂ distances ‖μ_i(Xₜ)−μ_i^S‖₂ and ‖σ_i(Xₜ)−σ_i^S‖₂ are summed over layers. Combining both objectives yields the fitness  
L(f_Θ(p; Xₜ)) = ∑_{x∈Xₜ}∑_{c∈C} −ŷ_c log ŷ_c + λ∑_{i=1}^N (‖μ_i(Xₜ)−μ_i^S‖₂ + ‖σ_i(Xₜ)−σ_i^S‖₂),  
where λ is a trade-off parameter. This formulation operates purely via forward passes, circumvents unstable learning signals in corrupted settings, and provides a reliable unsupervised objective for CMA-based prompt adaptation.

components.name: Back-to-Source Activation Shifting
components.description: Back-to-Source Activation Shifting is a forward-only mechanism that, during inference, directly modifies each test sample’s final-layer CLS token activation e_N^0 by shifting it along a direction designed to align out-of-distribution features with the source in-distribution manifold. Specifically, after obtaining e_N^0 via a standard forward pass (with the current prompt), the activation is updated as e_N^0 ← e_N^0 + γ d_t, where the shift direction d_t = μ_N^S − μ_N(t) is the vector difference between the source CLS activation center μ_N^S—computed once over a pool of source in-distribution samples—and the dynamically estimated test activation center μ_N(t). The test center μ_N(t) is maintained per batch through an exponential moving average, μ_N(t) = α μ_N(X_t) + (1−α) μ_N(t−1), in which μ_N(X_t) denotes the mean CLS activation of the t-th test batch and α∈[0,1] (set to 0.1) controls the update rate. The step size γ (commonly set to 1.0) determines how strongly each activation is pulled toward the source distribution. By applying this adjustment immediately before the final task head and without any gradient computation or weight updates, Back-to-Source Activation Shifting improves robustness to distributional shifts while preserving the model’s original parameters.

findings: Entropy-Only Fitness Collapse: Empirically, using only prediction entropy as the fitness for CMA prompt adaptation leads to collapsed or degenerate solutions and near-zero adaptation accuracy. The gradients of discrete quantized activations vanish, and noisy predictions at high corruption severities render entropy unreliable for black-box optimization.

Activation Discrepancy Regularization Effectiveness: Incorporating activation distribution discrepancy into the fitness stabilizes CMA prompt learning and substantially boosts OOD adaptation accuracy (from 55.5% to 63.4% on ImageNet-C) and reduces calibration error (ECE from 10.5% to 9.4%) without backpropagation.

Back-to-Source Activation Shifting Alone Yields Gains: Applying only the back-to-source activation shifting mechanism (without any prompt adaptation) to the final CLS activations still improves OOD accuracy (from 55.5% to 59.1%) and reduces ECE (from 10.5% to 12.7%), demonstrating its standalone effectiveness.

Robustness to Quantization, Non-IID, and ID Performance: The forward-only adaptation paradigm is compatible with 8-bit and 6-bit quantized ViT models—outperforming gradient-based TENT on full-precision models while reducing memory by up to 24×—and remains effective under non-IID test streams and class-imbalanced scenarios. Moreover, it preserves in-distribution accuracy (85.11% vs. 85.17%) with negligible forgetting.
